name: Route through the Serra do Cando and
name_original: Cotobade - vuelta de los Rios Lerez y Almofrei
description: This moderate 28.8-mile road cycling route in Cotobade explores the scenic Lerez and Almofrei Rivers, featuring historical bridges and natur
region_intro: Road cycling around Cotobade offers diverse landscapes, from scenic river valleys to challenging hilly terrain. The region is characterized by its riverine scenery along the Lérez, Almofrei, and Verdugo Rivers, often featuring historical bridges. Cyclists can explore a mix of rural environments and the more demanding climbs of areas like the Serra do Cando. This varied topography provides options for different cycling preferences and abilities. Best road cycling routes around Cotobade The most popular road cycling route is Route through the Serra do Cando and, a 28.8 miles (46.4 km) trail that takes 2 hours 26 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the Serra do Cando. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Borela Bridge loop from Pontevedra, a moderate 16.1 miles (26.0 km) path. This route offers scenic views along the river and passes by the historic Borela Bridge. Local road cyclists also love the Verdugo River Beach – Borela Bridge loop from Buchabade, a 74.6 miles (120.1 km) trail leading through river valleys and rural landscapes, often completed in about 6 hours 24 minutes. Road cycling around Cotobade is defined by its riverine scenery, diverse rural landscapes, and challenging hilly sections. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate rides to difficult routes with significant elevation. The routes in Cotobade are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 34 reviews. More than 3000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Cotobade's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Cotobade?
A: Cotobade offers a wide selection of road cycling routes, with over 180 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including easy, moderate, and difficult rides.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on road cycling routes in Cotobade?
A: The region provides diverse terrain, ranging from scenic river valleys along the Lérez, Almofrei, and Verdugo Rivers to challenging hilly sections in areas like the Serra do Cando. You'll encounter a mix of rural landscapes and some routes even feature historical bridges, adding a cultural dimension to your ride.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Cotobade?
A: Yes, Cotobade has routes suitable for various abilities. While many routes offer moderate to difficult challenges, there are also easier options, particularly those that follow river valleys. For example, routes along the Lérez and Almofrei Rivers tend to be less demanding and more suitable for a relaxed pace.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced riders in Cotobade?
A: Absolutely. Experienced cyclists looking for a challenge will find plenty of options, especially in the mountainous terrain. The Serra do Cando, for instance, features significant elevation gain. The broader Pontevedra area also includes climbs like the Alto da Groba, known for testing professional cyclists.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along road cycling routes in Cotobade?
A: Many routes in Cotobade pass by beautiful natural features and landmarks. You might encounter the stunning &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2030121" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Barosa Waterfall and Mills&lt;/a&gt;, the historic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5315365" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almofrei Stone Bridge&lt;/a&gt;, or the serene &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/488410" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Río Tomeza Riverside Trail&lt;/a&gt;. The region's riverine scenery along the Lérez and Almofrei Rivers is a constant highlight.

Q: Are there any historical sites or cultural points of interest near the cycling routes?
A: Yes, the area is rich in history and culture. Cyclists can discover the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4090129" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Outeiro Prehistoric Huts Ethnoarchaeological Park&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1508484" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Stonemason&lt;/a&gt;, or the iconic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3725313" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ponte do Burgo&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/975330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pontevedra Old Town&lt;/a&gt;, with its &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3969392" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of the Pilgrim Virgin&lt;/a&gt;, is also easily accessible from Cotobade.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes available in Cotobade?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes in Cotobade are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31793902" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Borela Bridge loop from Pontevedra&lt;/a&gt; and the longer &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38166107" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Verdugo River Beach – Borela Bridge loop from Buchabade&lt;/a&gt;, which offers extensive river valley and rural landscapes.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about road cycling in Cotobade?
A: The komoot community highly rates road cycling in Cotobade, with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 34 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, from scenic river valleys to challenging hilly sections, and the well-maintained network of routes that cater to various abilities.

Q: Is parking available for cyclists in Cotobade?
A: While specific parking areas for cyclists are not always signposted, you can typically find parking in the villages and towns within Cotobade, especially near popular starting points for routes. It's advisable to check local parking regulations upon arrival.

Q: Can I reach Cotobade's cycling routes using public transport?
A: Cotobade is located near Pontevedra, which has good public transport links. While direct public transport to every route starting point within Cotobade might be limited, you can often reach the general area by bus from Pontevedra. Cyclists often combine public transport to a nearby town with a short ride to their desired route.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Yes, as you cycle through the rural villages and towns of Cotobade and the surrounding areas, you'll find local cafes, bars, and restaurants where you can stop for refreshments. These establishments are often concentrated in the larger settlements or along main roads, providing convenient breaks during your ride.

Q: What is the best time to road cycle in Cotobade?
A: The region generally offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn provide mild temperatures and beautiful scenery, making them ideal. Summer can be warm, especially in the midday sun, but early mornings or late afternoons are still excellent for riding. Winters are typically mild, though some routes at higher elevations might be cooler.
tips: Borela Bridge, next to the hermitage of Lourdes, in Cotobade. Built over the Almofrei river, this bridge dates back to the 17th century, although we would go back to the 15th century since it was originally a medieval bridge
One of the tributaries of the Lérez river. Very nice recreational area along this small river with native forest passing by several mills, bridges with a river beach
Stone market building with annual gastronomy fair, with artisanal food and local products celebrating in the communities around Cerdedo - Cotobade

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: This tour is rated as moderate. While sections along the riverbanks offer relatively flat and tranquil riding, the broader Cerdedo-Cotobade region includes hilly and mountainous areas. Cyclists should be prepared for varied terrain, including some challenging climbs when moving away from the immediate river courses, requiring good fitness.

faqs.question: Where does the tour start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The tour is a loop, so you can start at various points. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, the region is rural. Look for parking in small villages or designated areas near historical bridges like the Borela Bridge, which are often good access points.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this road cycling route?
faqs.answer: You can expect a mix of terrain. The route follows the Lerez and Almofrei Rivers, offering relatively flat sections with gentle gradients along the riverbanks. However, the surrounding landscape of Cerdedo-Cotobade is rugged and hilly, meaning you will encounter significant climbs and descents, particularly when moving away from the immediate river valleys. Surfaces are generally well-paved for road cycling, though some local roads might be less maintained.

faqs.question: What natural features and landmarks can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: The route is rich in natural beauty and historical elements. You'll encounter lush riverine vegetation, traditional mills, and several ancient bridges, some dating back to the 14th century, such as Ponte de Parada, Ponte de Pedre, Ponte de Serrapio, and the Borela Bridge. The Almofrei Stone Bridge is particularly notable for its views. You'll also pass by the Maneses River and the Cuspedriños Market and Livestock Enclosure.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The best time to cycle this route is generally during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is typically milder and more pleasant for cycling, with less extreme heat than summer and fewer rainy days than winter. The riverine vegetation is also at its most vibrant.

faqs.question: Is the Cotobade - Tour of the Lerez and Almofrei Rivers dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route primarily uses public roads suitable for racebikes, which aren't typically designed for accompanying dogs, if you plan to stop and explore on foot, dogs are generally permitted in rural areas of Galicia. However, always keep your dog on a leash, especially near roads or in populated areas, and be mindful of local regulations and wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this area?
faqs.answer: No, you do not need specific permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route. The area is part of the ZEC SERRA DO CANDO, a protected natural area, but general road cycling on public roads does not require special permission. Always respect the natural environment and any local signage regarding conservation.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will find yourself on parts of the Sendeiro Rural de Galicia, the Mtb Vía Mariana, the Via Mariana, and the Senda do Lérez. It also passes near the Ponte Borela and Ponte de Ladróns trails, offering opportunities to explore further if you wish.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, pubs, or accommodation options along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through a rural region, so amenities are scattered. You'll find small cafes and local pubs in the villages you pass through. For accommodation, it's best to look for rural guesthouses (casas rurales) or small hotels in the larger towns nearby, such as Pontevedra or within the Cerdedo-Cotobade municipality.

faqs.question: What should I bring for this road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the moderate difficulty and varied terrain, bring appropriate road cycling gear, including a helmet, water bottles (as water sources may be limited), snacks, and basic repair tools. Layers of clothing are advisable due to potential changes in elevation and weather. A fully charged phone and a navigation device with the route downloaded are also recommended.

faqs.question: Are there any traffic-free sections on this route?
faqs.answer: Yes, parts of the route offer a pleasant, largely traffic-free experience. Some local roads and sections along the rivers are noted for being quiet, with one specific cycling route in the area mentioned as 99% traffic-free. However, as it's a road cycling tour, expect to share some segments with vehicular traffic, especially when passing through villages or on main connecting roads.
